input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, comprising a permanent magnet synchronous motor, gear transmission, and Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller for precise motion control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services including continuous multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction to enhance motion accuracy and real-time visualization via a dashboard for monitoring and analysis.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for multi-level abstraction (requirements, functional, logical, and technical layers), utilizing Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component & connector modeling. Behavior is described via MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, integrated with MathWorks Simulink and other simulation tools, general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java), and methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for managing system complexity across abstraction levels.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization via MQTT streaming of sensor time series data, integrating live data with white- and black-box friction models during operation for continuous digital shadow updates.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) implemented as a UML class diagram, comprising data traces, aggregation layers, and a Processing component that consumes input data traces and produces output data for specific manufacturing purposes.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration using Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized asset representation and MQTT protocol for real-time sensor data streaming with external systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) utilizes SysML for multi-layered system modeling across requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs describe components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral modeling employs MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, with detailed behavior analysis integrated via MathWorks Simulink, simulation tools, and general-purpose code (C++, Java).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data streamed via MQTT protocol in a one-way data flow from physical to digital; no details on transmission frequencies, event triggers, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, or integration mechanisms specified.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: No virtual-to-physical interaction exists as digital shadows function as passive entities with no data or command flow back to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol used for sensor data streaming; network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy mechanisms, and security protocols not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle stages are structured according to Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) abstraction levels, including requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer, as described in Section II.A. SysML is utilized for modeling these levels during development.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process follows a purpose-driven methodology consisting of four steps: (1) problem identification, (2) asset and model analysis, (3) use or creation of calculations/models, and (4) data identification. Validation is performed by comparing simulated data against measured data to derive friction parameters (Section IV.A). The system employs SysML for component & connector modeling (Fig. 3), and a pipeline facilitates dynamic exchange of friction models (Section I).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Digital shadows enable continuous identification of joint friction through model-based analytics, with insights visualized in a unified dashboard for real-time user interaction and decision support.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system complies with Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) standards, utilizing SysML for component & connector modeling as demonstrated in Fig. 3, ensuring interoperability with industry-specific modeling specifications.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description is highly accurate and specific to the text in Section III. It correctly identifies the robot as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', the drive mechanism as a 'permanent magnet synchronous motor' with 'gear transmission', and the controller as 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'. Quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'.

The extraction accurately reflects the services described in the Abstract and Section IV. It mentions 'continuous multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', 'enhance the robot’s motion accuracy', and 'visualization in a dashboard'. Quotes: 'resulting digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', 'ultimately improve robot motion accuracy', 'visualization in a dashboard'.

The extraction is highly detailed and matches the background section (Section II.A) perfectly. It correctly lists the MBSE methodologies, languages, and tools mentioned for the implementation. Supported by quotes: 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML', 'methodologies... e.g., RFLP... OOSEM... SPES, or SMArDT', 'MathWorks Simulink... other simulation tools... general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies real-time aspects, the specific protocol used (MQTT), and the integration of models during operation. Supported by quotes: 'real-time, data-driven representations', 'time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT', and 'combine white- and black-box friction models with live data during the operation'.

The source documents do not mention multiple digital twin instances, centralized versus decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between twins. The text focuses on a single digital shadow for a specific robot axis. No relevant evidence is present.

The description correctly captures the DSRM, its implementation as a UML diagram, and the role of the Processing component. Supported by quotes: 'Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) as a Unified Modeling Language (UML) class diagram', 'a set of contextual data traces', and 'Processing component... consumes (uses) input data traces and produces output data'.

The extraction correctly identifies Asset Administration Shells and MQTT as key integration tools. Supported by quotes: 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' and 'streamed via MQTT'. However, phrases like 'standardized asset representation' and 'with external systems' are slight interpretations of the text rather than direct quotes, making it a 'Good' but not perfect extraction.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately summarizes the system's capabilities. The text states the digital shadows 'enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' (Abstract) and involve 'visualization in a dashboard' (Abstract, Section I). It further notes that 'integrated visualization within a unified dashboard' allows for a 'better understanding of the physical relationships' (Section I). This supports the extraction regarding continuous identification of joint friction and insights visualization for decision support.

The extraction accurately describes the system's adherence to standards. The text states that 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML' (Section II.A). It also confirms the use of SysML for modeling: 'Fig. 3 shows the robot’s structure as a component & connector SysML model' (Section IV.A). The extraction is well-supported by the provided sources.
